keyTakeaways: The standard Part B premium for 2026 is $202.90 per month, up from $185 in 2025
Higher earners face IRMAA surcharges, with premiums reaching $689.90 monthly for individual income above $500,000

Original Medicare has no annual out-of-pocket maximum, leaving you responsible for 20% coinsurance indefinitely

seoTitle: Complete Medicare Plan B Guide: 2026 Costs & Coverage
seoDescription: Complete Medicare Plan B Guide covering 2026 costs, coverage details, and tips to choose the right supplemental plan with confidence.

sections.text: Understanding Medicare Part B: A Clear Starting Point

sections.content: Medicare Part B pays for the medical care you receive outside a hospital: doctor visits, outpatient procedures, and preventive screenings. It sounds simple, but the details around premiums, deductibles, and enrollment windows can turn a straightforward benefit into a source of stress.

You are not the only one who finds Medicare's alphabet of parts and plans confusing. This guide walks through what Part B covers, what it costs in 2026, and how to decide whether you need additional coverage alongside it.

If you are turning 65 soon or reviewing coverage you already have, the 2026 premium and deductible changes are worth understanding before you make any decisions. Small cost shifts can affect your monthly budget more than they first appear to.

sections.text: What Medicare Part B Actually Covers

sections.content: Part B covers medically necessary outpatient care. That includes visits to your primary doctor, specialist appointments, lab work, outpatient surgery, ambulance transport, and durable medical equipment like walkers, wheelchairs, and oxygen equipment.

Outpatient mental health services are also included, such as counseling and psychiatric visits that don't require a hospital stay. Part B works alongside Part A, which covers inpatient hospital stays, skilled nursing facility care, and hospice. Together, they form Original Medicare.

sections.calloutText: Part B generally does not cover routine dental, vision, hearing aids, or most prescription drugs. Those gaps are why many people pair Part B with a Medicare Advantage plan, Part D plan, or Medigap policy.

sections.content: Knowing what falls outside Part B helps you avoid an unpleasant surprise at the pharmacy counter or the dentist's office. It also helps you evaluate whether you need supplemental coverage from day one.

sections.text: Preventive Services You May Not Know Are Included

sections.content: Part B covers an annual wellness visit at no cost to you, along with many common screenings: mammograms, colonoscopies, diabetes screenings, and cardiovascular checks, among others. These preventive benefits carry no coinsurance or deductible when you see a provider who accepts Medicare assignment.
Many beneficiaries never use these benefits simply because they don't know they exist. Understanding what's included helps you get full value from the coverage you're already paying for each month.

sections.text: 2026 Part B Costs: Premiums, Deductibles, and Out-of-Pocket Expenses

sections.content: The standard Part B premium for 2026 is $202.90 per month, up from $185 in 2025. Most people pay this standard amount, but if your income is above certain thresholds, you'll pay an Income-Related Monthly Adjustment Amount, known as IRMAA, on top of it.

sections.title: 2026 Part B Costs at a Glance
sections.footnote: IRMAA surcharges apply above certain income thresholds based on your 2024 tax return.

sections.content: Once you meet the $283 annual deductible in 2026, Medicare generally pays 80% of the Medicare-approved amount for covered services. You're responsible for the remaining 20% coinsurance, and unlike many private insurance plans, Original Medicare has no annual out-of-pocket maximum.

sections.calloutText: Higher earners can pay significantly more. Individual income above $500,000 (or $750,000 for joint filers) pushes the Part B premium to $689.90 per month in 2026. Check where your income falls before you budget for coverage.

sections.content: That open-ended 20% coinsurance is exactly why many people pair Part B with a Medigap policy or choose a Medicare Advantage plan instead. A Medigap plan like Plan G can cover most of that coinsurance, trading an unpredictable expense for a steady monthly premium.

sections.text: Why Costs Feel Uncertain Without a Side-by-Side Comparison

sections.content: Your real cost of care is never just the premium. It's the premium plus the deductible plus the coinsurance you'll actually pay based on how often you see doctors or need procedures.

A plan with a lower premium can end up costing more over a year if it has higher copays or a higher deductible. Comparing plans side by side, using your actual expected care, gives you a far more accurate picture than comparing premiums alone.

I always tell people to add up their total expected costs for the year, not just the monthly premium, before choosing between Original Medicare with a supplement and Medicare Advantage. A $30 difference in premium can look small, but it can flip the math entirely once you factor in coinsurance, copays, and how often you actually see the doctor.

sections.text: Will Your Doctors and Prescriptions Be Covered?

sections.content: With Original Medicare, including Part B, you can generally see any doctor or specialist in the country who accepts Medicare assignment. There's no network to navigate and no referral required to see a specialist in most cases.
Medicare Advantage plans work differently. They typically use provider networks, similar to private insurance, which means your favorite specialist might be in-network one year and out the next.

Prescription drugs are mostly handled separately. Part B covers some drugs administered in a clinical setting, like infusions or injections, but most medications you pick up at a pharmacy fall under Part D or a Medicare Advantage plan with drug coverage built in.

items: Ask your doctor's office directly whether they accept Medicare assignment
Check the plan's provider directory if you're considering Medicare Advantage
Review a plan's drug formulary to confirm your specific prescriptions are covered
Compare formulary tiers, since the same drug can cost differently across plans

sections.calloutText: Call your doctor's billing office and ask them to confirm Medicare participation in writing or note it in your file. It takes a few minutes and can prevent a costly misunderstanding later.

sections.text: Enrollment Timing: Deadlines, Penalties, and What to Watch For

sections.content: Your Initial Enrollment Period spans seven months: it starts three months before the month you turn 65, includes your birthday month, and extends three months after. Enrolling during this window helps you avoid gaps in coverage and late penalties.

If you miss that window and don't qualify for a Special Enrollment Period, you'll need to wait for the General Enrollment Period, which runs January 1 to March 31 each year, with coverage starting the following month.

sections.calloutText: If you delay Part B enrollment without qualifying coverage, your premium can increase by 10% for each 12-month period you were eligible but didn't enroll. This penalty is added to your premium for as long as you have Part B.

sections.content: If you're still working and covered by an employer group health plan, you may qualify for a Special Enrollment Period that lets you delay Part B without penalty. This depends on the size of your employer and the type of coverage, so it's worth confirming your specific situation before assuming you're safe to wait.

sections.text: Signs It Is Time to Take Action This Year

sections.content: Receiving enrollment paperwork in the mail is a clear signal that your window is open and worth reviewing closely. If you're turning 65 within the next few months, mapping out your enrollment timeline now gives you room to compare options without rushing.
Waiting until the last few weeks of your enrollment period tends to create unnecessary pressure. Starting early, even just to gather information, puts you back in control of the decision.

sections.text: Comparing Your Options Without Feeling Overwhelmed

sections.content: Broadly, you're choosing between two paths: Original Medicare (Parts A and B) paired with a Medigap policy and separate Part D plan, or a Medicare Advantage plan that bundles hospital, medical, and often drug coverage into one plan.

sections.title: Original Medicare + Medigap vs. Medicare Advantage

sections.content: A simple framework helps cut through the noise: list your current doctors, your prescriptions, your monthly budget, and how much care you expect to need this year. Then compare plans against that list rather than against a generic ranking.

Writing your priorities down before you start comparing plans reduces the chance of getting swayed by a flashy benefit you'll never use. If your list still leaves you uncertain, that's a reasonable point to bring in a licensed Medicare agent for a second set of eyes.

sections.text: Getting Help You Can Trust With Your Part B Decisions

sections.content: Licensed Medicare agents are trained to walk through your specific doctors, medications, and budget, then show you how different plans stack up against those needs. A good conversation should feel like a plain-language explanation, not a pitch for one particular plan.

Transparent guidance means the agent tells you what plans are available in your area, explains the tradeoffs clearly, and never pressures you to decide on the spot. You should walk away with more clarity, not more confusion.

sections.calloutTitle: What a No-Obligation Comparison Looks Like
sections.calloutText: A licensed agent reviews your doctors, prescriptions, and budget, then compares available plans in your area. You decide whether to enroll, and there's no requirement to commit during the call.

sections.content: Real client reviews and testimonials can also help you feel more confident before reaching out. Seeing how the process worked for someone in a similar situation often makes the first conversation feel far less intimidating.

sections.text: Frequently Asked Questions About Medicare Part B

faqs.question: I am not ready to enroll yet. Can I take more time before deciding?
faqs.answer: Your Initial Enrollment Period gives you a seven-month window, so there is usually time to gather information before deciding. Just be aware that missing this window without other coverage can trigger a late enrollment penalty, so it helps to know your exact deadline early.

faqs.question: Will contacting someone about Part B lead to constant sales calls?
faqs.answer: A licensed agent conversation should focus on answering your questions and comparing plans, not repeated follow-up calls. You can ask upfront how the agent will contact you and set boundaries on communication before the conversation even begins.

faqs.question: Can I figure out Part B coverage on my own with more research?
faqs.answer: Yes, self-research is possible, and many people start there. It can take considerable time to compare plan details, formularies, and networks accurately, which is where a licensed agent often adds the most value by narrowing choices down quickly.

faqs.question: How do I know which sources of Medicare information to trust?
faqs.answer: Look for plain-language explanations, licensed agent credentials, and clear disclosures about plan availability. It also helps to cross-check what you're told against official resources like Medicare.gov or the CMS website.

faqs.question: What if I choose a plan and later regret the decision?
faqs.answer: Medicare offers annual enrollment periods where you can review and change your coverage. Reassessing your plan each year is normal and expected, so an early choice doesn't have to lock you in permanently.

faqs.question: Does Part B cover services if I see a specialist outside my network?
faqs.answer: Original Medicare, including Part B, does not use networks, so you can generally see any specialist who accepts Medicare assignment. Medicare Advantage plans are different and often restrict coverage to in-network providers, so confirm participation before your appointment.

sections.content: Understanding what Part B covers, comparing the real costs, and confirming your doctors and prescriptions are the three steps that matter most. Getting these right early saves you from surprises down the road.
Enrollment deadlines matter, but making an informed decision matters more than rushing to beat a date. Take the time you need to compare your options clearly.
When you're ready, a pressure-free conversation with a licensed Medicare agent can help confirm you're on the right track, or point out something you may have missed.
